era_context: The late Renaissance in Europe; the Council of Trent in its final sessions. The Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent continues its dominance. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty rules China; Japan remains fragmented during the Sengoku period. The Age of Exploration continues with European colonial expansion. Religious conflict intensifies in Europe following the Protestant Reformation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions and archaeological evidence underrepresented)
Central Asia and Siberia (sparse written records)

events.title: Treaty of Edinburgh ends the Auld Alliance between Scotland and France
events.description: The Treaty of Edinburgh, signed on 6 July 1560, formally ended the Auld Alliance between Scotland and France, a military and political alliance that had lasted for centuries. The treaty was negotiated between Scottish Protestant nobles, the English government, and French representatives. The treaty's provisions included the withdrawal of French troops from Scotland and the recognition of Scottish independence from French control. The treaty marked a significant shift in Scottish politics, as Scotland moved away from its traditional alliance with France and toward closer ties with England. The treaty also effectively ended the regency of Mary of Guise, the mother of Mary, Queen of Scots, and paved the way for the establishment of Protestantism as the dominant religion in Scotland.
events.certainty_note: The treaty's date and provisions are well-documented in primary sources.
cross_references: 1561-003 (Mary, Queen of Scots returns to Scotland)

events.title: Scottish Parliament establishes Protestantism as the official religion
events.description: In August 1560, the Scottish Parliament passed legislation establishing Protestantism as the official religion of Scotland and abolishing papal authority. The legislation prohibited Catholic worship and established the Church of Scotland as a Protestant church. The religious settlement was a significant moment in Scottish history, as Scotland became one of the first European nations to formally establish Protestantism as the state religion. The religious settlement was driven by Scottish Protestant nobles who had gained power following the Treaty of Edinburgh. The establishment of Protestantism in Scotland would have profound consequences for Scottish society and politics for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The Scottish Parliament's establishment of Protestantism is well-documented in parliamentary records.

events.title: Death of Francis II of France
events.description: Francis II, King of France, died on 5 December 1560 at age 16. Francis had been king for only 17 months, having succeeded his father Henry II in 1559. His death marked the end of the brief reign of a sickly young king and the beginning of a period of regency under his mother, Catherine de Medici. Francis' death also had significant consequences for his widow, Mary, Queen of Scots, who would return to Scotland in 1561 following her husband's death. The death of Francis II marked a turning point in French politics, as Catherine de Medici would become the dominant figure in French governance during the reigns of her remaining sons.
key_figures: Mary, Queen of Scots (widow of Francis II)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Francis II's death

events.certainty_note: Francis II's death on 5 December 1560 is well-documented in contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1561-003 (Mary, Queen of Scots returns to Scotland)

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Süleyman the Magnificent's continued dominance
events.description: By 1560, Süleyman I (r. 1520–1566) was in his final years but remained the dominant figure in the Ottoman Empire and the Mediterranean. Süleyman's reign had expanded Ottoman territory to its greatest extent and established Ottoman cultural and legal institutions that would endure for centuries. In 1560, Süleyman was consolidating his legacy and preparing for his succession. His continued authority demonstrated the Ottoman Empire's ongoing dominance in the region, though his advanced age (he was 66 years old) meant that succession planning was becoming increasingly important.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and chronicles (Peçevi, Mustafa Selaniki)

events.certainty_note: Süleyman's continued dominance in 1560 is documented in general terms, though specific events and policies may vary in sources.

events.title: Spain: Philip II consolidates control over the Spanish Empire
events.description: By 1560, Philip II (r. 1556–1598) had consolidated his control over the Spanish Empire, which included Spain, the Spanish Netherlands, parts of Italy, and vast territories in the Americas. Philip was establishing Madrid as the capital and planning the construction of the Escorial Palace as a symbol of Spanish power and Catholic orthodoxy. His reign was marked by religious zeal, military ambition, and administrative centralisation. Philip's policies would shape European politics for decades, including his support for the Counter-Reformation and his conflicts with Protestant powers.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Philip II's reign

events.certainty_note: Philip II's consolidation of power was a gradual process; 1560 marks a significant point in his reign, though no single dramatic event is documented for this specific year.

events.title: Ivan the Terrible: Livonian War begins
events.description: In 1558, Ivan IV (Ivan the Terrible) began the Livonian War, seeking to expand Muscovite control over the Baltic region. By 1560, the war was well underway, with Ivan conducting military campaigns against the Livonian Order and other Baltic powers. The Livonian War (1558–1583) was a prolonged conflict between Muscovy and various Baltic powers (the Livonian Order, Poland-Lithuania, Sweden). By 1560, Ivan had achieved some military successes, but the war was becoming increasingly costly and difficult. The Livonian War would ultimately end in Muscovite defeat and the loss of Baltic territories, contributing to the destabilisation of Ivan's later reign.

sources.name: Kurbsky, Prince Andrei. History of Ivan IV (contemporary account, written in exile)

events.certainty_note: Ivan's military campaigns in Livonia during 1560 are documented in general terms, though specific battles and dates may vary in sources.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Jiajing Emperor's administrative decline
events.description: By 1560, the Jiajing Emperor (r. 1521–1567) was in his final years, and the Ming Dynasty's administrative apparatus was showing signs of strain. The emperor had withdrawn from active governance, delegating authority to eunuchs and officials. Corruption and factional conflicts within the bureaucracy were increasing. The Jiajing Emperor's reign, which had begun with promise, was ending in administrative decline. His successor, the Longqing Emperor, would attempt to reform the system through capable administrators like Zhang Juzheng.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of administrative decline in the Jiajing Emperor's final years is well-documented, though specific events in 1560 are not individually highlighted in sources.

events.title: Akbar's Mughal Empire: Consolidation of northern India
events.description: By 1560, Akbar (r. 1556–1605) had consolidated his control over much of northern India through military campaigns and administrative reforms. Akbar's policy of religious tolerance and administrative centralisation distinguished his rule from earlier sultanates. His reign marked the height of Mughal power and cultural achievement, with significant developments in art, architecture, and literature. Akbar's policies of religious pluralism and meritocratic administration would become defining features of Mughal governance.

sources.name: Abul Fazl. Akbarnama (official court history, compiled 1590s)

events.certainty_note: Akbar's consolidation was a gradual process; 1560 marks a significant point in his reign, though no single dramatic event is documented for this specific year.

events.title: Elizabeth I of England: Consolidation of Protestant settlement
events.description: By 1560, Elizabeth I (r. 1558–1603) had consolidated the Protestant settlement in England. The religious settlement established the Church of England as the official state church with the monarch as its supreme head. Elizabeth's religious policy was pragmatic and aimed at reducing religious conflict while maintaining royal supremacy over the church. Her settlement represented a middle way between Catholicism and radical Protestantism. The Thirty-Nine Articles, which would formally define Anglican doctrine, were being developed and would be adopted in 1563.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Elizabeth I's religious settlement

events.certainty_note: Elizabeth's religious settlement was a gradual process; 1560 marks a significant point in its development, though the Thirty-Nine Articles were formally adopted in 1563.

events.title: Council of Trent: Continuation of doctrinal definitions
events.description: In 1560, the Council of Trent was in its sessions, working to define Catholic doctrine in response to the Protestant Reformation. The council was addressing issues of clerical discipline, the nature of the sacraments, and the role of tradition in the Church. The council's work would be completed in 1563 with the final decrees. The Council of Trent represented a comprehensive Catholic response to Protestantism and would shape Catholic theology and practice for centuries.

sources.name: Concilium Tridentinum: Diariorum, Actorum, Epistolarum, Tractatuum collectio (official council records)
Jedin, Hubert. History of the Council of Trent (1957–1961)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Trent's sessions in 1560 are documented in official council records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Edinburgh is sometimes portrayed as a complete victory for Scottish Protestants, but modern scholarship emphasises that it was a negotiated settlement that reflected the balance of power between Scottish, English, and French interests. The treaty's provisions regarding French withdrawal were not immediately implemented, and French influence in Scotland persisted for some time. The Scottish Parliament's establishment of Protestantism is sometimes presented as a unanimous decision, but in fact, there was significant opposition from Catholic nobles and the Catholic Church. The religious settlement was driven by a coalition of Protestant nobles who had gained power following the Treaty of Edinburgh. Francis II's death is sometimes attributed to various causes (including poisoning), but modern historians generally accept that he died of natural causes, likely related to an ear infection or similar illness. The Council of Trent's work in 1560 is sometimes presented as a unified Catholic response to Protestantism, but in fact, there was significant debate within the council about the extent of reform and the proper relationship between the papacy and national churches.
historiographic_note: The year 1560 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for England, Scotland, France, Italy, and the Ottoman Empire. Western European archives provide rich documentation of political, religious, and cultural events. However, documentation for Asia (China, Japan, India) relies heavily on later compilations and official chronicles rather than contemporary primary sources. The bias toward European and Mediterranean history in surviving sources is pronounced. Additionally, the dating of events in non-Gregorian calendar systems (Islamic, Chinese, Hebrew) introduces potential ambiguities when converting to the proleptic Gregorian calendar used here.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Edinburgh in July 1560 paved the way for the Scottish Parliament's establishment of Protestantism in August 1560.
The Scottish Parliament's establishment of Protestantism in 1560 created a hostile religious environment for Mary, Queen of Scots when she returned to Scotland in 1561.
Francis II's death in December 1560 prompted Mary, Queen of Scots to return to Scotland in 1561.
The Council of Trent's sessions in 1560 led to the conclusion of the council in 1564.
